citation: Schubert, T., Friedmann, F., & Regenbrecht, H. (2001). The experience of presence: Factor analytic insights. Presence: Teleoperators and Virtual Environments, 10(3), 266-281.
intended_use: Virtual environments (immersive VR, desktop VR, 3D games, text-based VEs)

general_presence.label: General Presence (GP)

items.text: In the computer generated world I had a sense of 'being there'.

general_presence.note: Single global item; used as criterion validity check; not summed into subscale total

spatial_presence.label: Spatial Presence (SP)

items.text: I had a sense of acting in the virtual space.
I felt present in the virtual space.
I did not feel present in the virtual space.
I had a sense of being there.
The virtual world seemed more realistic than the real world.

spatial_presence.scoring: Mean of items after reverse-scoring SP3

involvement.label: Involvement (INV)

items.text: I was not aware of my real environment.
I still paid attention to the real environment.
I was completely captivated by the virtual world.
My experience in the virtual environment seemed consistent with my real world experience.

involvement.scoring: Mean of items after reverse-scoring INV2

realness.label: Realness (REAL)

items.text: The virtual world seemed real to me.
Somehow I felt that the virtual world was not real.
Objects in the virtual environment did not seem real to me.

realness.scoring: Mean of items after reverse-scoring REAL2 and REAL4

scoring_instructions.method: Subscale means; higher scores indicate greater presence/realness/involvement
scoring_instructions.validation_notes: GP item used as external criterion; all subscales should correlate positively with GP

theoretical_grounding: Zahorik & Jenison (1998) presence-as-being-in-the-world; Schubert et al. factor analysis of 75-item pool
